questions.q: What is the difference between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP?

answer.oneLiner: DELETE removes rows one at a time and logs every row individually, which means you can filter with a WHERE clause and it fires triggers. TRUNCATE is much faster because it deallocates whole data pages instead of logging each row, but you cannot use a WHERE clause and it resets the identity seed. DROP goes further still — it removes the entire table object, including its structure, indexes, and constraints, not just the data.
keyPoints: DELETE is a DML statement that logs every row deletion, fires triggers, and supports a WHERE clause to target specific rows.
TRUNCATE is a DDL statement that uses minimal logging by deallocating pages, cannot filter with WHERE, and resets the IDENTITY seed.
DROP removes the entire table structure along with all its data, indexes, and constraints — the table no longer exists afterward.
Both DELETE and TRUNCATE can be rolled back if they run inside an explicit transaction.

code.note: DELETE supports WHERE; TRUNCATE does not

answer.analogy: DELETE tears pages out one by one; TRUNCATE recycles all pages but keeps the cover; DROP shreds the whole book.
redFlags: Saying TRUNCATE can never be rolled back
Confusing TRUNCATE and DROP
Forgetting DELETE fires triggers

dialogue.text: If I need to clear a staging table fast before a nightly load, what would you use?
`TRUNCATE TABLE` — it deallocates the pages instead of logging row-by-row, so it's much faster and it also resets the IDENTITY seed.
And if I only wanted to remove last year's rows?
Then `DELETE ... WHERE OrderDate < '2024-01-01'`, because `TRUNCATE` can't take a `WHERE` clause and is all-or-nothing.
Is `TRUNCATE` safe inside a transaction — can I roll it back?
Yes, both `DELETE` and `TRUNCATE` are fully transactional and roll back inside an explicit transaction; `DROP` removes the whole table object, structure and all.

followUpsQA.q: Can TRUNCATE be rolled back?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, if it's inside an explicit transaction it rolls back like any DML.

followUpsQA.q: Why doesn't TRUNCATE fire triggers?
followUpsQA.a: It deallocates pages instead of deleting rows, so per-row DML triggers never see the rows.

followUpsQA.q: What happens to the IDENTITY column after each?
followUpsQA.a: TRUNCATE resets the seed, DELETE keeps it, DROP removes the column entirely.

followUpsQA.q: Why is TRUNCATE blocked when a foreign key references the table?
followUpsQA.a: SQL Server can't validate FK references page-by-page, so it disallows TRUNCATE on referenced tables.

questions.q: Explain INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, and FULL OUTER JOIN with examples.

answer.oneLiner: Think of two overlapping circles: an INNER JOIN gives you only the overlap — rows that match in both tables. A LEFT JOIN gives you the entire left circle, filling in NULLs where there is no match on the right. A FULL OUTER JOIN combines both circles, so you get all rows from both sides with NULLs wherever a match is missing.
keyPoints: INNER JOIN returns only the rows where the ON condition matches in both tables — non-matching rows are excluded entirely.
LEFT JOIN returns every row from the left table and fills in NULLs for the right-side columns wherever there is no match.
RIGHT JOIN is the mirror of a LEFT JOIN — every row from the right table is kept, with NULLs for unmatched left columns.
FULL OUTER JOIN returns all rows from both tables, placing NULLs on whichever side has no matching row.
Adding WHERE right.key IS NULL after a LEFT JOIN creates an anti-join that returns only the rows with no match on the right.

code.snippet:
-- Sample tables: Customers(CustomerId, Name), Orders(OrderId, CustomerId, Total)

-- INNER: only customers who have orders
SELECT c.Name, o.Total
FROM Customers c
INNER JOIN Orders o ON c.CustomerId = o.CustomerId;

-- LEFT: all customers, NULL Total where no order exists
SELECT c.Name, o.Total
FROM Customers c
LEFT JOIN Orders o ON c.CustomerId = o.CustomerId;

-- Anti-join: customers with NO orders
SELECT c.Name
FROM Customers c
LEFT JOIN Orders o ON c.CustomerId = o.CustomerId
WHERE o.CustomerId IS NULL;

-- FULL OUTER: all rows from both tables
SELECT c.Name, o.Total
FROM Customers c
FULL OUTER JOIN Orders o ON c.CustomerId = o.CustomerId;
code.note: Anti-join pattern (LEFT + WHERE IS NULL) is a classic interview question

answer.analogy: Two Venn circles: INNER is the overlap, LEFT/RIGHT is one whole circle, FULL OUTER is both circles combined.
redFlags: Confusing LEFT and RIGHT semantics
Not knowing the anti-join pattern
Thinking FULL OUTER duplicates rows

dialogue.text: I want every customer listed even if they've never ordered. Which join?
A `LEFT JOIN` from Customers to Orders — you keep all customer rows and get NULLs for the order columns where there's no match.
Now flip it: just the customers with no orders at all.
Same LEFT JOIN plus `WHERE o.CustomerId IS NULL` — that's the anti-join pattern that keeps only the unmatched left rows.
What does FULL OUTER JOIN add over those?
It returns unmatched rows from *both* sides at once — all customers and all orphan orders, with NULLs filling the gaps on either side.

followUpsQA.q: What is a CROSS JOIN and when would you use it?
followUpsQA.a: A Cartesian product of both tables — useful for generating combinations like date x region grids.

followUpsQA.q: How does a SELF JOIN work?
followUpsQA.a: You join a table to itself with aliases, e.g. employees to their managers in the same table.

followUpsQA.q: JOIN vs subquery performance?
followUpsQA.a: Usually equivalent — the optimizer often rewrites one into the other; readability decides.

followUpsQA.q: How is an anti-join written?
followUpsQA.a: LEFT JOIN plus WHERE right.key IS NULL, or NOT EXISTS.

questions.q: How do you optimize a slow SQL query, and how do you read an execution plan?

answer.oneLiner: The first thing I do is turn on SET STATISTICS IO so I have a baseline of logical reads — that tells me how much I/O the query is actually doing. Then I pull the actual execution plan and read it right to left, looking for thick arrows that indicate a large number of rows flowing through, table scans instead of seeks, Key Lookups where the index is not covering, and any implicit conversion warnings. Once I know the bottleneck I fix it — usually by adding a covering index, rewriting a non-SARGable predicate, or updating stale statistics — and then I measure again to confirm the improvement.
keyPoints: Always establish a baseline using SET STATISTICS IO and SET STATISTICS TIME ON, because logical reads are the most reliable measure of I/O cost.
Read graphical execution plans from right to left, and treat the thickness of the arrows as a visual signal of how many rows are flowing between operators.
The most expensive signals to watch for are Table Scans, Key Lookups, Sort operators, and Hash Match joins on large inputs.
Non-SARGable predicates such as wrapping a column in a function, selecting all columns with SELECT star, and implicit type conversions all prevent the optimizer from doing an index seek.
Missing index hints in the plan are a useful starting point but not gospel — always validate them against the real workload before creating an index.
answer.detail: Key Lookup means a non-clustered index seek had to go back to the clustered index for extra columns; fix with a covering index (INCLUDE). Make predicates SARGable by keeping functions off indexed columns.

code.note: Always benchmark with SET STATISTICS IO to confirm improvement

redFlags: Adding indexes without reading the plan first
Using NOLOCK as a default performance fix
Not knowing what a Key Lookup is

dialogue.text: A report query suddenly takes 30 seconds. Walk me through your first moves.
I'd turn on `SET STATISTICS IO, TIME ON` to get a baseline on logical reads, then pull the actual execution plan and read it right-to-left.
What in the plan catches your eye?
Thick arrows meaning lots of rows, plus Table Scans, Key Lookups, or a big Sort — and any green warning for an implicit conversion.
You see an Index Seek followed by a Key Lookup. Fix?
Add a covering index — keep the seek key and `INCLUDE` the extra columns so the lookup back to the clustered index disappears; then I re-measure IO to confirm.

followUpsQA.q: What is parameter sniffing and how do you deal with it?
followUpsQA.a: A cached plan built from the first parameters; fix with RECOMPILE, OPTIMIZE FOR, or Query Store.

followUpsQA.q: When would you use OPTION (RECOMPILE)?
followUpsQA.a: When parameter values vary widely and a single cached plan hurts some calls.

followUpsQA.q: How does Query Store help with regressions?
followUpsQA.a: It tracks plan history so you can spot a regression and force the prior good plan.

followUpsQA.q: What makes a predicate non-SARGable?
followUpsQA.a: Wrapping the indexed column in a function or causing an implicit conversion.

questions.q: What is a CTE (Common Table Expression) and when would you use one?

answer.oneLiner: A CTE is a named temporary result set you define with the WITH keyword, and it exists only for the duration of the single statement that follows it — think of it like naming an intermediate calculation on a whiteboard that you erase when you are done. The main reasons to use one are readability, because the query reads top-down in logical steps, and recursion, which lets you walk parent-child hierarchies like an org chart without looping in application code. Unlike a temp table it is not a physical object, so the optimizer usually inlines it rather than materializing it.
keyPoints: The syntax starts with WITH followed by a name and the query in parentheses, then a single SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE that references it.
A CTE is scoped to one statement and is not a physical temp table — it lives only in the query plan.
Recursive CTEs have two parts joined by UNION ALL: an anchor member that seeds the recursion and a recursive member that joins back to the CTE itself, which makes them ideal for hierarchies.
The default recursion limit is 100 iterations and you can override it with OPTION MAXRECURSION followed by the number you want.
answer.detail: A non-recursive CTE is usually inlined by the optimizer, not materialized, so referencing it many times can re-execute it; use a #temp table if that's expensive.

code.snippet:
-- Non-recursive: top customers by order value
WITH CustomerTotals AS (
    SELECT CustomerId, SUM(Total) AS TotalSpent
    FROM Orders
    GROUP BY CustomerId
)
SELECT c.Name, ct.TotalSpent
FROM CustomerTotals ct
JOIN Customers c ON c.CustomerId = ct.CustomerId
ORDER BY ct.TotalSpent DESC;

-- Recursive: org-chart hierarchy
WITH OrgChart AS (
    -- Anchor: CEO (no manager)
    SELECT EmployeeId, Name, ManagerId, 0 AS Level
    FROM Employees
    WHERE ManagerId IS NULL

    UNION ALL

    -- Recursive: reports
    SELECT e.EmployeeId, e.Name, e.ManagerId, oc.Level + 1
    FROM Employees e
    INNER JOIN OrgChart oc ON e.ManagerId = oc.EmployeeId
)
SELECT * FROM OrgChart
OPTION (MAXRECURSION 50);
code.note: Recursive CTEs require UNION ALL — UNION is not allowed in the recursive member

answer.analogy: A CTE is like naming an intermediate calculation on a whiteboard, then erasing it when done.
redFlags: Thinking a CTE is persisted like a temp table
Not knowing about recursive CTEs
Claiming CTEs always improve performance

dialogue.text: When do you reach for a CTE instead of a subquery?
Mostly for readability — I name an intermediate result with `WITH` so the final query reads top-down, and for recursion over hierarchies.
Is a CTE materialized like a temp table?
No — a non-recursive CTE is usually inlined by the optimizer, so if I reference it many times it can re-execute each time.
So when would you switch to a #temp table?
When that intermediate result is expensive and reused multiple times — a #temp materializes once and even gets statistics.

followUpsQA.q: CTE vs temp table?
followUpsQA.a: CTE is inlined and single-statement; temp table is materialized in tempdb with stats.

followUpsQA.q: Can a CTE be indexed?
followUpsQA.a: No — it's not a physical object; index the underlying table or use a #temp.

followUpsQA.q: What if a recursive CTE never terminates?
followUpsQA.a: It hits MAXRECURSION (default 100) and errors; you can raise it with OPTION (MAXRECURSION n).

followUpsQA.q: Why must a recursive CTE use UNION ALL?
followUpsQA.a: UNION's distinct step isn't allowed in the recursive member.

questions.q: What is a temporary table? What is the difference between #temp, ##temp, and a table variable (@table)?

answer.oneLiner: A local temp table prefixed with a single hash sign lives in tempdb and is private to your session, so it disappears when your connection closes or you drop it explicitly. A global temp table prefixed with two hash signs is also in tempdb but is visible to every session until the session that created it disconnects. A table variable declared with at sign is scoped only to the current batch or stored procedure and has no real statistics, which means the optimizer almost always guesses just one row regardless of how much data is actually in it.
keyPoints: A local temp table is session-scoped in tempdb, supports additional indexes and proper statistics, and participates in transaction rollbacks just like a regular table.
A global temp table is visible to all sessions and persists in tempdb until the creating session disconnects — use it carefully in production.

A table variable is scoped to the current batch or procedure, has no statistics so the optimizer assumes a single row, and its changes are not rolled back when the surrounding transaction is rolled back.
For small result sets a table variable is fine, but for larger intermediate sets a local temp table is the better choice because the optimizer can use real statistics to build a good plan.
answer.detail: Because table variables have no statistics, the optimizer estimates 1 row and can pick bad plans on large data. Their rollback immunity makes them handy for audit logging inside TRY/CATCH.

code.note: Table variables cannot have CREATE INDEX added after declaration; temp tables can

redFlags: Saying table variables behave just like temp tables
Not knowing ##temp is visible across sessions
Claiming table variables are always faster

dialogue.text: Difference between a #temp table and a @table variable?
#temp lives in tempdb, is session-scoped, and gets real statistics; a @table variable is batch-scoped and the optimizer assumes it has one row because it has no stats.
So why would you ever pick the table variable?
For small sets, and because it's immune to transaction rollback — handy for audit logging inside a TRY/CATCH that you still want to persist.
And what's `##temp`?
A global temp table visible to all sessions until the creating session disconnects — I generally avoid it in production.

followUpsQA.q: Can a temp table be accessed from another connection?
followUpsQA.a: Only a global ##temp; a local #temp is private to its session.

followUpsQA.q: Why don't table variables roll back?
followUpsQA.a: They're scoped to the batch and treated outside the user transaction for rollback purposes.

followUpsQA.q: When choose a CTE over a temp table?
followUpsQA.a: For single-statement readability or recursion where materializing isn't needed.

followUpsQA.q: Why can a table variable pick a bad plan on big data?
followUpsQA.a: No statistics, so the optimizer estimates one row and may choose nested loops.

questions.q: What is a trigger? How do you create and use one in SQL Server?

answer.oneLiner: A trigger is a special stored procedure that SQL Server fires automatically whenever a DML event like an insert, update, or delete happens on a table — you do not call it yourself. Inside the trigger body you have access to two virtual tables called INSERTED and DELETED that hold the new and old row states respectively, which lets you capture exactly what changed. Triggers run inside the same transaction as the statement that fired them, so a ROLLBACK inside a trigger will undo the caller's work too.
keyPoints: An AFTER trigger, which is the default type, fires after the DML statement completes and you can still issue a ROLLBACK from within it to cancel the entire operation.
An INSTEAD OF trigger replaces the DML statement entirely rather than running after it, which is commonly used to make non-updatable views support updates.
INSERTED holds the new rows for inserts and updates, while DELETED holds the old rows for deletes and updates — on an UPDATE both tables are populated simultaneously.
Triggers always run in the same transaction as the triggering statement, and because INSERTED and DELETED are sets you must always write set-based logic to handle multi-row operations correctly.
answer.detail: A ROLLBACK inside a trigger aborts the whole calling transaction. Keep triggers minimal — they are invisible to app developers and hard to debug.

code.snippet:
-- AFTER INSERT trigger: log new orders to an audit table
CREATE OR ALTER TRIGGER trg_Orders_AfterInsert
ON Orders
AFTER INSERT
AS
BEGIN
    SET NOCOUNT ON;
    INSERT INTO OrderAudit (OrderId, CustomerId, Total, AuditAction, AuditDate)
    SELECT OrderId, CustomerId, Total, 'INSERT', GETUTCDATE()
    FROM INSERTED;  -- INSERTED can have multiple rows
END;

-- INSTEAD OF UPDATE trigger on a view
CREATE OR ALTER TRIGGER trg_vCustomerOrders_InsteadOfUpdate
ON vCustomerOrders
INSTEAD OF UPDATE
AS
BEGIN
    SET NOCOUNT ON;
    UPDATE o SET o.Total = i.Total
    FROM Orders o
    INNER JOIN INSERTED i ON o.OrderId = i.OrderId;
END;
code.note: Always handle multi-row: INSERTED is a table, not a single row

redFlags: Assuming INSERTED/DELETED hold exactly one row
Not knowing a ROLLBACK in a trigger rolls back the caller
Overusing triggers for business logic

dialogue.text: How would you audit every change to an Orders table automatically?
An AFTER trigger on Orders that reads the `INSERTED` and `DELETED` virtual tables and writes the old/new values into an audit table.
One gotcha people miss in triggers?
Assuming INSERTED holds a single row — it's a set, so a multi-row UPDATE breaks any code that uses scalar logic.
When would you use INSTEAD OF instead of AFTER?
To make a view updatable or to substitute the DML entirely — INSTEAD OF replaces the operation rather than running after it.

followUpsQA.q: AFTER vs INSTEAD OF triggers?
followUpsQA.a: AFTER runs post-DML; INSTEAD OF replaces it, commonly to update views.

followUpsQA.q: Can triggers be recursive/nested?
followUpsQA.a: Yes — controlled by the RECURSIVE_TRIGGERS and nested triggers server options.

followUpsQA.q: How would you audit changes without a trigger?
followUpsQA.a: Use temporal tables, Change Data Capture, or change tracking.

followUpsQA.q: What happens if a trigger issues ROLLBACK?
followUpsQA.a: It aborts the entire calling transaction, not just the trigger's work.

questions.q: What is an index? Explain the difference between a clustered and a non-clustered index.

answer.oneLiner: An index is a B-tree data structure that lets SQL Server find rows quickly without reading every page in the table — think of it like the index at the back of a textbook. A clustered index is special because its leaf level actually is the table data, physically ordered by the key, so you can only have one per table. A non-clustered index is a separate structure that stores the key columns plus a pointer back to the actual row, and you can have up to 999 of them.
keyPoints: A table with no clustered index is called a heap, meaning the rows have no physical order and are located by a row identifier.
The leaf level of a clustered index holds the actual data rows in key order — the index and the table are the same physical structure.
The leaf level of a non-clustered index holds the index key plus a row locator, which is either the clustered key or a row identifier on a heap.

A Key Lookup occurs when a non-clustered index seek finds the key but needs additional columns that are not in the index — it then has to jump back to the clustered index for those columns, which adds extra I/O per row.
Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes because every insert, update, and delete must also maintain each index on the table.

code.snippet:
-- Default: PRIMARY KEY creates a clustered index
CREATE TABLE Orders (
    OrderId   INT IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,  -- clustered by default
    CustomerId INT NOT NULL,
    OrderDate  DATE NOT NULL,
    Total      DECIMAL(18,2)
);

-- Non-clustered index on CustomerId
CREATE NONCLUSTERED INDEX IX_Orders_CustomerId
    ON Orders (CustomerId);

-- Covering index: include Total to avoid Key Lookup for this common query
CREATE NONCLUSTERED INDEX IX_Orders_Customer_Covering
    ON Orders (CustomerId)
    INCLUDE (OrderDate, Total);

-- Explicit clustered index (different column)
CREATE CLUSTERED INDEX CIX_Orders_OrderDate
    ON Orders (OrderDate);  -- table physically sorted by date
code.note: Only one clustered index per table; choose the key wisely as it affects all non-clustered indexes

answer.analogy: Clustered = phone book sorted by last name (data itself is ordered). Non-clustered = the index at the back of a book pointing to page numbers.
redFlags: Saying you can have multiple clustered indexes
Not knowing what a Key Lookup is
Ignoring the write overhead of indexes

dialogue.text: Clustered versus non-clustered index — the core difference?

A clustered index *is* the table — its leaf level holds the actual rows in key order, so there's only one per table. A non-clustered index is a separate structure with the key plus a locator back to the row.
What's a heap?
A table with no clustered index — rows are unordered and non-clustered indexes locate them by RID instead of the clustered key.
A non-clustered seek needs a column it doesn't have. What happens?
A Key Lookup back to the clustered index for the missing columns — I'd fix it with a covering index that INCLUDEs them.

followUpsQA.q: What is a covering index?
followUpsQA.a: One that holds every column a query needs, eliminating the Key Lookup.

followUpsQA.q: Why are GUIDs poor clustered keys?
followUpsQA.a: Random values cause page splits and fragmentation on insert.

followUpsQA.q: What is index fragmentation and how do you fix it?
followUpsQA.a: Out-of-order pages; fix with REORGANIZE (5-30%) or REBUILD (>30%).

followUpsQA.q: Do indexes slow down writes?
followUpsQA.a: Yes — each index must be maintained on insert, update, and delete.

questions.q: Write a query to find the Nth highest salary from an Employees table.

answer.oneLiner: The cleanest solution is to use DENSE_RANK ordered by salary descending inside a CTE and then filter where the rank equals N, because DENSE_RANK handles ties correctly by giving the same rank to equal salaries without skipping numbers. Using ROW_NUMBER instead would be wrong when multiple employees share the same salary, because it would arbitrarily assign them different numbers and could exclude a valid result from the Nth position. Window functions are evaluated after the WHERE clause, so you always need to wrap them in a CTE or subquery before you can filter on the result.
keyPoints: DENSE_RANK is the preferred function for this problem because ties receive the same rank and no rank numbers are skipped, so the Nth highest salary is always correct even when values repeat.
ROW_NUMBER assigns a unique sequential number to every row regardless of ties, which means it can return the wrong result when multiple employees share a salary.
Window functions are computed in the SELECT phase, after the WHERE clause is evaluated, so you cannot filter on a window function directly in WHERE — you must wrap it in a CTE or subquery first.
An alternative approach using a correlated subquery with COUNT DISTINCT works but is slower and harder to read than the window function approach.

code.snippet:
-- Method 1: DENSE_RANK in a CTE (preferred)
WITH RankedSalaries AS (
    SELECT
        EmployeeId,
        Name,
        Salary,
        DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS SalaryRank
    FROM Employees
)
SELECT EmployeeId, Name, Salary
FROM RankedSalaries
WHERE SalaryRank = 3;  -- Change 3 to any N

-- Method 2: ROW_NUMBER (wrong for ties, but interviewer may ask for the distinction)
WITH Numbered AS (
    SELECT EmployeeId, Name, Salary,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS RowNum
    FROM Employees
)
SELECT * FROM Numbered WHERE RowNum = 3;

-- Method 3: Classic correlated subquery (for comparison only)
SELECT MIN(Salary)
FROM (
    SELECT DISTINCT TOP 3 Salary
    FROM Employees
    ORDER BY Salary DESC
) AS Top3;
code.note: Always clarify tie-handling with the interviewer — DENSE_RANK is the safe default

redFlags: Using ROW_NUMBER without noting it differs on ties
Unable to write it with a window function
Forgetting window functions can't go in WHERE

dialogue.text: Write me the 3rd highest salary. How do you start?
I'd put `DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Salary DESC)` in a CTE and then filter `WHERE SalaryRank = 3` — it handles ties correctly.
Why not ROW_NUMBER?
ROW_NUMBER gives every row a unique number, so if two people tie for second it would wrongly skip one of them for the Nth position.
Why wrap it in a CTE at all?
Because a window function can't be referenced in the WHERE clause directly — it's computed after WHERE, so I need the CTE or a subquery to filter on it.

followUpsQA.q: Difference between RANK, DENSE_RANK, ROW_NUMBER?
followUpsQA.a: ROW_NUMBER is unique, RANK gaps after ties, DENSE_RANK no gaps.

followUpsQA.q: Nth highest salary per department?
followUpsQA.a: Add PARTITION BY Department to the window function.

followUpsQA.q: Can you do it without a window function?
followUpsQA.a: Yes — a correlated subquery or TOP with an ORDER BY, but it's slower.

followUpsQA.q: Why can't window functions go in WHERE?
followUpsQA.a: They're logically evaluated after WHERE, in the SELECT phase.

questions.q: What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING, and when do you use GROUP BY?

answer.oneLiner: WHERE and HAVING both filter rows, but they operate at different points in query execution. WHERE runs before any grouping happens and filters individual rows, so you cannot use aggregate functions like SUM or COUNT inside it. HAVING runs after GROUP BY has collapsed the rows into groups, so it is the right place to filter based on aggregate results — for example, keeping only customers who placed more than five orders.
keyPoints: WHERE is evaluated before GROUP BY runs, which means aggregate functions are not allowed in a WHERE clause.
HAVING is evaluated after GROUP BY and is specifically designed to filter grouped results using aggregate functions.
The logical processing order is FROM, then WHERE, then GROUP BY, then HAVING, then SELECT, and finally ORDER BY — which is why a SELECT alias is not visible in a WHERE clause.
As a performance best practice, always filter rows in WHERE as early as possible so fewer rows flow into the GROUP BY operation.

code.snippet:
-- Customers who placed more than 5 orders with a total spend > $1000
SELECT
    c.Name,
    COUNT(o.OrderId)      AS OrderCount,
    SUM(o.Total)          AS TotalSpent
FROM Customers c
INNER JOIN Orders o ON c.CustomerId = o.CustomerId
WHERE o.OrderDate >= '2023-01-01'       -- WHERE: filter rows first
GROUP BY c.CustomerId, c.Name           -- GROUP BY: collapse into groups
HAVING COUNT(o.OrderId) > 5            -- HAVING: filter groups by aggregate
   AND SUM(o.Total) > 1000
ORDER BY TotalSpent DESC;
code.note: The WHERE clause here removes old orders BEFORE grouping — more efficient than filtering after

redFlags: Putting aggregates in WHERE instead of HAVING
Not knowing the logical processing order
Omitting a non-aggregate column from GROUP BY

dialogue.text: WHERE versus HAVING — when do you use each?
WHERE filters individual rows before grouping; HAVING filters the groups after aggregation, so aggregates like `SUM` or `COUNT` can only go in HAVING.
Does it matter which one cuts the rows?
Yes — filter as much as possible in WHERE first so fewer rows reach the GROUP BY; it's more efficient than aggregating then discarding.
What's the logical processing order?
FROM, then WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, SELECT, then ORDER BY — which is why a SELECT alias isn't visible in WHERE.

followUpsQA.q: Can you use a SELECT alias in WHERE?
followUpsQA.a: No — WHERE runs before SELECT, so the alias doesn't exist yet.

followUpsQA.q: What does HAVING without GROUP BY do?
followUpsQA.a: It treats the whole result as one group and filters on the aggregate.

followUpsQA.q: COUNT(*) vs COUNT(column)?
followUpsQA.a: COUNT(*) counts all rows; COUNT(column) ignores NULLs in that column.

followUpsQA.q: Why must non-aggregated SELECT columns be in GROUP BY?
followUpsQA.a: Each group returns one row, so every output column must be an aggregate or a grouping key.

questions.q: Explain window functions — ROW_NUMBER, RANK, and DENSE_RANK — with examples.

answer.oneLiner: Window functions let you compute values across a related set of rows while keeping every individual row in the result, unlike GROUP BY which collapses rows into summaries. The OVER clause defines the window — PARTITION BY resets the calculation for each group and ORDER BY determines the sort order within each partition. ROW_NUMBER always produces a unique sequential number with no ties, RANK gives tied rows the same number but then skips ahead leaving a gap, and DENSE_RANK also gives tied rows the same number but never skips so there are no gaps.
keyPoints: Unlike GROUP BY, window functions do not collapse rows — every detail row is preserved in the output alongside the computed window value.
The OVER clause is required and defines the window: PARTITION BY divides rows into independent groups and ORDER BY determines the ranking or frame order within each group.

ROW_NUMBER assigns a unique sequential integer to every row, RANK assigns the same number to ties but then skips numbers to account for the ties, and DENSE_RANK assigns the same number to ties without skipping any numbers afterward.
Other useful window functions include LAG and LEAD for accessing adjacent rows, SUM and AVG over a frame for running totals, and NTILE for dividing rows into equal-sized buckets.
answer.detail: Common use: de-dupe with ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY key ORDER BY date DESC) and keep rn = 1.

code.snippet:
SELECT
    Name,
    Department,
    Salary,
    ROW_NUMBER()  OVER (PARTITION BY Department ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS RowNum,
    RANK()        OVER (PARTITION BY Department ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS Rnk,
    DENSE_RANK()  OVER (PARTITION BY Department ORDER BY Salary DESC) AS DenseRnk
FROM Employees;

-- Result for Engineering dept with salaries 90k, 85k, 85k, 70k:
-- RowNum: 1, 2, 3, 4
-- Rank:   1, 2, 2, 4   (skips 3)
-- DenseRnk: 1, 2, 2, 3 (no skip)

-- De-duplicate: keep latest record per CustomerId
WITH Deduped AS (
    SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY CustomerId ORDER BY UpdatedAt DESC) AS rn
    FROM CustomerEvents
)
SELECT * FROM Deduped WHERE rn = 1;
code.note: De-duplication with ROW_NUMBER is one of the most common real-world uses

redFlags: Confusing RANK and DENSE_RANK gap behavior
Not knowing window functions keep all rows
Trying to use them in WHERE

dialogue.text: What problem do window functions solve that GROUP BY doesn't?
They compute across related rows without collapsing them — so I keep every detail row and still add a rank or running total alongside it.
Spell out RANK versus DENSE_RANK on ties.
With two rows tied at first, RANK gives 1,1 then skips to 3, while DENSE_RANK gives 1,1 then 2 with no gap; ROW_NUMBER would just be 1,2.
A real-world use you reach for often?
De-duplication: `ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY key ORDER BY date DESC)` then keep `rn = 1` to grab the latest record per key.

followUpsQA.q: ROWS vs RANGE in a window frame?
followUpsQA.a: ROWS counts physical rows; RANGE groups peers with equal ORDER BY values.

followUpsQA.q: LAG() vs a self-join?
followUpsQA.a: LAG reads the prior row directly — simpler and faster than a self-join.

followUpsQA.q: Can window functions be used in WHERE or GROUP BY?
followUpsQA.a: No — wrap them in a CTE or subquery to filter on the result.

followUpsQA.q: What does PARTITION BY do?
followUpsQA.a: Resets the window calculation for each group, like a per-group ranking.

questions.q: What is a covering index and how does it eliminate a Key Lookup?

answer.oneLiner: A covering index is a non-clustered index that contains every column a particular query needs, so the database engine can satisfy the entire query from the index alone without going back to the base table. You achieve this by putting the columns you search, filter, or sort on as key columns, and then adding any extra columns needed only in the SELECT list using the INCLUDE clause. When the index does not cover all needed columns, the optimizer performs a Key Lookup for each row — an extra round-trip to the clustered index that adds significant I/O on large result sets.
keyPoints: A Key Lookup is the extra I/O cost incurred per row when the non-clustered index has to jump back to the clustered index to fetch columns that are not in the index.
The INCLUDE clause adds columns at the leaf level of the index only, without including them in the B-tree sorting structure, which keeps the index smaller while still covering the query.
Put a column in the key position if the query seeks, range-scans, or sorts on it; put a column in INCLUDE if it is only needed in the SELECT list.
The trade-off is that covering indexes are larger and every write to the table must maintain those included columns in the index too.
answer.detail: Put a column in the key if you seek, range-scan, or sort on it; use INCLUDE if it's only needed in SELECT. Check sys.dm_db_missing_index_details for suggestions, but validate against the real workload.

code.snippet:
-- Query that causes a Key Lookup on IX_Orders_CustomerId
SELECT OrderId, OrderDate, Total
FROM Orders
WHERE CustomerId = 42;
-- Execution plan shows: Index Seek (IX_Orders_CustomerId) -> Key Lookup

-- Fix: covering index
CREATE NONCLUSTERED INDEX IX_Orders_CustomerId_Covering
    ON Orders (CustomerId)          -- key: seek happens here
    INCLUDE (OrderDate, Total);     -- leaf only: eliminates Key Lookup

-- Find missing index suggestions
SELECT
    mid.statement AS TableName,
    mid.equality_columns,
    mid.include_columns,
    migs.avg_user_impact
FROM sys.dm_db_missing_index_details mid
JOIN sys.dm_db_missing_index_groups mig ON mid.index_handle = mig.index_handle
JOIN sys.dm_db_missing_index_group_stats migs ON mig.index_group_handle = migs.group_handle
ORDER BY migs.avg_user_impact DESC;
code.note: INCLUDE columns are stored only at leaf level — they don't affect seek efficiency but eliminate Key Lookups

redFlags: Not knowing what a Key Lookup is
Putting every column in the key instead of INCLUDE
Blindly applying every missing index suggestion

dialogue.text: What is a covering index?
One that contains every column a query touches — key columns plus INCLUDE columns — so the query is satisfied entirely from the index with no trip back to the table.
How does that relate to a Key Lookup?
A Key Lookup is the extra I/O per row when the index lacks a needed column; covering it with INCLUDE removes those lookups.
Key column or INCLUDE — how do you decide where a column goes?
Key if I seek, range-scan, or sort on it; INCLUDE if it only appears in the SELECT list, since INCLUDE lives at the leaf only.

followUpsQA.q: Key column vs INCLUDE column?
followUpsQA.a: Key columns drive seeks/sorts; INCLUDE columns only provide coverage at the leaf.

followUpsQA.q: How many indexes on a write-heavy table?
followUpsQA.a: As few as the workload needs — each one adds write and storage overhead.

followUpsQA.q: What is index intersection?
followUpsQA.a: The optimizer combines two non-covering indexes to satisfy a query.

followUpsQA.q: Where do missing-index suggestions come from?
followUpsQA.a: sys.dm_db_missing_index_* DMVs — useful hints, but validate before applying.

questions.q: What causes a table scan vs an index seek? When does the optimizer choose one over the other?

answer.oneLiner: The optimizer chooses between a scan and a seek based on cost, and several things can force it into a scan: there is no suitable index, the predicate is not SARGable so the B-tree cannot be navigated efficiently, the query matches so many rows that a scan is cheaper than thousands of Key Lookups, or the statistics are stale and the optimizer underestimates the row count. It picks a seek when the predicate is SARGable, a good index exists, and the selectivity is high enough that navigating the B-tree saves more work than it costs.
keyPoints: An index seek navigates the B-tree directly to the matching rows and is efficient when only a small fraction of rows match the predicate.
A table or index scan reads every row in the table and is chosen when selectivity is low, no index exists, or the optimizer decides the seek plus Key Lookup cost exceeds the scan cost.

A predicate is SARGable when the optimizer can use it to navigate the index tree — wrapping a column in a function, using a leading wildcard like percent at the start, or causing an implicit type conversion all break SARGability.
The optimizer relies on statistics to estimate row counts, and stale statistics can cause it to choose the wrong plan by underestimating or overestimating how many rows will be returned.
Above roughly one to three percent of rows, the cost of an index seek plus a Key Lookup for each row can actually exceed a straight table scan, so the optimizer may prefer the scan.
answer.detail: Implicit type conversion (VARCHAR column vs NVARCHAR parameter) converts every row and forces a scan — it shows as a warning in the plan. Keep statistics updated.

code.snippet:
-- Non-SARGable: function on column causes scan
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE YEAR(OrderDate) = 2024;  -- BAD

-- SARGable equivalent: allows index seek on OrderDate
SELECT * FROM Orders
WHERE OrderDate >= '2024-01-01' AND OrderDate < '2025-01-01';  -- GOOD

-- Implicit conversion (scan warning in plan if col is VARCHAR)
DECLARE @id NVARCHAR(10) = N'123';
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE OrderId = @id;  -- type mismatch may prevent seek

-- Check column statistics
DBCC SHOW_STATISTICS ('Orders', 'IX_Orders_CustomerId');

-- Update stale statistics
UPDATE STATISTICS Orders WITH FULLSCAN;
code.note: Green warnings in the execution plan often indicate implicit conversions causing index scans

redFlags: Not knowing what SARGable means
Thinking an index always means a seek
Ignoring statistics as a cause of bad plans

dialogue.text: When does the optimizer scan instead of seek?
When there's no suitable index, the predicate isn't SARGable, selectivity is low, or the statistics are stale and mislead the estimate.
Give me a non-SARGable example.
`WHERE YEAR(OrderDate) = 2024` — the function on the column blocks a seek; I'd rewrite it as a date range `>= '2024-01-01' AND < '2025-01-01'`.
Even with a good index, when might a scan win?
When the query returns a large fraction of rows — past roughly 1-3%, a scan often beats a seek plus thousands of Key Lookups.

followUpsQA.q: What is parameter sniffing?
followUpsQA.a: A cached plan built from the first parameter values that may not fit later calls.

followUpsQA.q: How (and when not) to force an index seek?
followUpsQA.a: Use an index hint as a last resort; it can backfire when data distribution changes.

followUpsQA.q: What is a filtered index?
followUpsQA.a: A non-clustered index with a WHERE clause covering only a subset of rows.

followUpsQA.q: How does an implicit conversion force a scan?
followUpsQA.a: Mismatched types convert every row, defeating the index seek.

questions.q: What is the difference between UNION and UNION ALL?

answer.oneLiner: UNION combines the results of two queries and then removes duplicate rows, which requires an extra sort or hash operation that can be expensive on large result sets. UNION ALL simply stacks all the rows together without any deduplication, making it noticeably faster. In practice you should default to UNION ALL and only use UNION when you genuinely need duplicates removed.
keyPoints: Both UNION and UNION ALL require that the queries on each side have the same number of columns with compatible data types.
UNION performs an implicit DISTINCT across the combined result, which adds a blocking sort or hash operation and makes it slower than UNION ALL.
UNION ALL keeps all rows including duplicates and does no extra work, so it is the faster choice whenever the two result sets are known to be disjoint or duplicates are acceptable.
An ORDER BY clause can only appear once at the very end of the entire UNION or UNION ALL statement, applying to the combined result.

code.snippet:
-- UNION: deduplicates; slower
SELECT CustomerId FROM ActiveCustomers
UNION
SELECT CustomerId FROM ArchivedCustomers;

-- UNION ALL: keeps duplicates; faster — use when sets are disjoint
SELECT OrderId, Total, 'Online' AS Channel FROM OnlineOrders
UNION ALL
SELECT OrderId, Total, 'Store'  AS Channel FROM StoreOrders
ORDER BY Total DESC;  -- ORDER BY goes at the end only
code.note: Default to UNION ALL unless you specifically need deduplication

redFlags: Defaulting to UNION when UNION ALL suffices
Not knowing why UNION is slower
Putting ORDER BY mid-chain

dialogue.text: UNION or UNION ALL — what's the difference?
UNION removes duplicates with an extra sort/hash step; UNION ALL keeps everything and skips that work, so it's faster.
Which should be your default?
UNION ALL — I only use plain UNION when I actually need de-duplication, otherwise I'm paying for a blocking step I don't need.
Where does ORDER BY go in a UNION chain?
Only once, at the very end — it applies to the combined result, not the individual SELECTs.

followUpsQA.q: UNION vs JOIN?
followUpsQA.a: UNION stacks rows vertically; JOIN combines columns horizontally on a key.

followUpsQA.q: How do INTERSECT and EXCEPT work?
followUpsQA.a: INTERSECT returns common rows; EXCEPT returns rows in the first set not in the second.

followUpsQA.q: When does UNION cause a performance issue?
followUpsQA.a: On large result sets where the distinct sort/hash becomes expensive.

followUpsQA.q: Do both sides need matching columns?
followUpsQA.a: Yes — same column count and compatible data types.

questions.q: What is the difference between a stored procedure and inline (ad-hoc) SQL?

answer.oneLiner: Stored procedures are named database objects that get their execution plan compiled and cached on the first run, and they give you a clean security boundary because you can grant execute permission without giving direct table access. Parameterized inline SQL using sp_executesql gets exactly the same plan caching benefit and is equally safe against injection, because in both cases values are passed as parameters rather than concatenated into the query string. The real injection risk is not inline SQL in general but string concatenation, where user input is directly joined into the query text.
keyPoints: Stored procedures compile and cache their execution plan on the first run and reuse that plan for subsequent calls, just like parameterized inline SQL does.
Parameterized inline SQL executed through sp_executesql is both plan-cached and safe from SQL injection, making it functionally equivalent to a stored procedure for those two concerns.
Stored procedures provide a useful security boundary by allowing you to grant EXECUTE permission on the procedure without giving the caller direct SELECT or UPDATE rights on the underlying tables.
String-concatenated SQL is the actual injection risk, regardless of whether it appears in a stored procedure or an ad-hoc statement — the danger is concatenation, not inline SQL itself.
answer.detail: Both stored procs and sp_executesql are subject to parameter sniffing — the plan is built from the first parameters seen. Mitigate with OPTION (RECOMPILE), OPTIMIZE FOR, or Query Store.

code.note: sp_executesql with parameters is as safe as a stored procedure for injection purposes

redFlags: Thinking stored procs are always faster than parameterized SQL
Not knowing about parameter sniffing
Thinking any stored proc prevents injection (not if it concatenates strings)

dialogue.text: Are stored procedures faster than inline SQL?
Not inherently — parameterized inline SQL via `sp_executesql` gets the same plan caching. The real difference is security boundaries and maintainability.
So is inline SQL the injection risk people warn about?
Only string-concatenated SQL is the risk; parameterized inline SQL is as safe as a proc because values are passed as parameters, not concatenated.
Any caching downside they share?
Both are subject to parameter sniffing since the plan is built from the first parameters — I'd mitigate with RECOMPILE, OPTIMIZE FOR, or Query Store.

followUpsQA.q: What is parameter sniffing and how do you deal with it?
followUpsQA.a: Plan built from first parameters; fix with RECOMPILE, OPTIMIZE FOR, or plan forcing.

followUpsQA.q: Inline table-valued function vs stored procedure?
followUpsQA.a: An inline TVF is inlined and composable in queries; a proc isn't queryable.

followUpsQA.q: How does EF Core handle parameterization?
followUpsQA.a: It parameterizes automatically, so generated SQL is injection-safe.

followUpsQA.q: What security benefit do stored procs give?
followUpsQA.a: GRANT EXECUTE on the proc without granting direct table access.

questions.q: What are ACID properties? Explain each in the context of SQL Server transactions.

answer.oneLiner: ACID is the set of four guarantees that make database transactions reliable — Atomicity means a transaction either fully commits or fully rolls back with no partial results, Consistency means the database always moves from one valid state to another, Isolation means concurrent transactions do not interfere with each other, and Durability means that once a transaction commits the data survives even a system crash. SQL Server implements these through write-ahead logging, which hardens log records to disk before the commit returns, and through its locking and isolation level system.
keyPoints: Atomicity means all statements in a transaction succeed together or all are rolled back — there is no such thing as a partial commit.
Consistency means that after every transaction the database remains in a valid state, enforced by constraints, triggers, and the business logic written inside the transaction.
Isolation is controlled by isolation levels and locking, and it determines how much of a transaction's intermediate state is visible to other concurrently running transactions.
Durability is guaranteed by the write-ahead log, which ensures that the log record for every committed transaction is hardened to disk before the commit acknowledgment is returned to the caller.
answer.detail: SET XACT_ABORT ON makes any runtime error roll back the whole transaction, not just the failing statement — use it with TRY/CATCH in stored procedures.

code.note: XACT_ABORT ON ensures the transaction rolls back on any error, not just the failing statement

answer.analogy: Like an ATM withdrawal: you get cash and the balance drops together (Atomic), account stays valid (Consistent), others aren't affected mid-way (Isolated), and the record is permanent once confirmed (Durable).
redFlags: Confusing Isolation with the other properties
Not knowing about the Write-Ahead Log
Omitting TRY/CATCH and XACT_ABORT

dialogue.text: Walk me through ACID with a bank transfer.

Atomicity means the debit and credit both commit or both roll back; Consistency keeps constraints valid; Isolation stops other transactions seeing the half-done state; Durability means once committed it survives a crash.
How is Durability actually guaranteed?
Write-Ahead Logging — the log record is hardened to disk before commit returns, so recovery can redo or undo after a crash.
In a stored proc, how do you make sure a mid-transaction error doesn't leave it half-applied?
`SET XACT_ABORT ON` with a TRY/CATCH that rolls back if `@@TRANCOUNT > 0` and re-throws — any error aborts the whole transaction.

followUpsQA.q: ROLLBACK vs SAVEPOINT?
followUpsQA.a: ROLLBACK undoes the whole transaction; SAVEPOINT rolls back to a marked point.

followUpsQA.q: How is durability ensured during a power failure?
followUpsQA.a: The write-ahead log is flushed before commit, so recovery replays it.

followUpsQA.q: What is XACT_ABORT and why use it?
followUpsQA.a: It rolls back the entire transaction on any runtime error, not just the failing statement.

followUpsQA.q: What enforces the Consistency property?
followUpsQA.a: Constraints, triggers, and the transaction's own logic keep the DB in a valid state.

questions.q: What are SQL Server isolation levels, and what concurrency problems does each prevent?

answer.oneLiner: SQL Server offers five isolation levels that trade concurrency for protection against three classic problems: dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, and phantom reads. Read Uncommitted offers no protection at all, Read Committed is the default and prevents dirty reads, Repeatable Read additionally prevents non-repeatable reads, and both Serializable and Snapshot prevent all three including phantoms. For most OLTP applications the best practical choice is enabling Read Committed Snapshot Isolation at the database level, because it gives readers a consistent view of committed data without taking shared locks that would block writers.
keyPoints: A dirty read means reading uncommitted data from another transaction that might still be rolled back — this can only happen at the Read Uncommitted level.

A non-repeatable read means reading the same row twice within one transaction and getting different values because another transaction committed a change in between — this is prevented at Repeatable Read and above.

A phantom read means running the same range query twice and getting different rows because another transaction inserted or deleted rows that match the range — this is prevented at Serializable and Snapshot.

Read Committed Snapshot Isolation changes the default Read Committed behavior so that readers see the last committed row version instead of taking shared locks, eliminating reader-writer blocking without any application code changes.

Snapshot Isolation versions all reads at the start of the transaction so the entire transaction sees a consistent point-in-time view, and it detects write conflicts when two transactions try to update the same row.
answer.detail: RCSI (READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT ON) makes readers not block writers without app changes. Snapshot Isolation versions at transaction start and throws a conflict error on concurrent updates.

code.note: Enable RCSI on Azure SQL / SQL Server to dramatically reduce blocking without changing application code

redFlags: Recommending NOLOCK without explaining the dangers
Not knowing dirty/non-repeatable/phantom reads
Confusing RCSI with Snapshot Isolation

dialogue.text: Name the isolation levels and what they prevent.
Read Uncommitted allows dirty reads; Read Committed (the default) stops those; Repeatable Read stops non-repeatable reads; Serializable and Snapshot also stop phantoms.
Readers are blocking writers in our OLTP system. Quick win?
Turn on RCSI — readers then see the last committed row version with no shared locks, so they stop blocking writers, and no app changes are needed.
How is that different from Snapshot isolation?
RCSI versions per-statement, while Snapshot versions for the whole transaction and throws a write-conflict error on concurrent updates.

followUpsQA.q: RCSI vs Snapshot Isolation?
followUpsQA.a: RCSI is statement-level versioning; Snapshot is transaction-level with conflict detection.

followUpsQA.q: Why is NOLOCK dangerous?
followUpsQA.a: It allows dirty reads and can return duplicated or missing rows during page splits.

followUpsQA.q: How does Snapshot detect write conflicts?
followUpsQA.a: It errors if another transaction modified a row it read since the snapshot began.

followUpsQA.q: What is a phantom read?
followUpsQA.a: A re-run range query returns new rows inserted by another transaction.

questions.q: What causes a deadlock in SQL Server and how do you prevent or resolve it?

answer.oneLiner: A deadlock happens when two or more transactions are each waiting for a lock that the other is holding, creating a circular wait that nobody can break on their own. SQL Server's lock monitor detects this cycle automatically and kills the transaction with the least expensive rollback cost, which receives error number 1205. The best prevention strategies are always accessing tables in the same order across all transactions, keeping transactions as short as possible, and using good indexes so that locks cover the fewest rows necessary.
keyPoints: SQL Server's lock monitor detects circular wait cycles automatically and chooses the cheapest transaction to kill as the deadlock victim, which receives error 1205.
The most effective prevention technique is always accessing shared tables in a consistent order across all transactions, because this prevents the circular wait from forming.
Keeping transactions short and committing as quickly as possible reduces the window during which locks are held and lowers the chance of overlap with competing transactions.

Good indexes reduce the number of rows that need to be locked during a scan, which shrinks the scope of potential conflicts, and enabling Read Committed Snapshot Isolation removes reader-writer deadlocks entirely.
Application code should catch error 1205 and retry the transaction with an appropriate backoff delay, since deadlocks are an expected condition in a concurrent system.
answer.detail: The system_health Extended Events session captures deadlock graphs by default; open the .xdl to see who held and waited on what. EF Core's EnableRetryOnFailure handles transient retries.

code.snippet:
-- Deadlock scenario:
-- Session A: UPDATE Orders SET...  then UPDATE Customers SET...
-- Session B: UPDATE Customers SET... then UPDATE Orders SET...
-- Fix: both sessions access tables in the SAME order

-- Capture deadlocks with Extended Events (query the ring buffer)
SELECT
    xdr.value('@timestamp', 'datetime2') AS DeadlockTime,
    xdr.query('.') AS DeadlockGraph
FROM (
    SELECT CAST(target_data AS XML) AS TargetData
    FROM sys.dm_xe_session_targets t
    JOIN sys.dm_xe_sessions s ON t.event_session_address = s.address
    WHERE s.name = 'system_health' AND t.target_name = 'ring_buffer'
) AS Data
CROSS APPLY TargetData.nodes('//RingBufferTarget/event[@name="xml_deadlock_report"]') AS XEventData(xdr)
ORDER BY DeadlockTime DESC;

-- Application retry for error 1205
-- (C# pseudocode: catch SqlException ex when ex.Number == 1205 => retry with backoff)
code.note: system_health Extended Events session captures deadlock graphs by default — no setup required

redFlags: Thinking NOLOCK solves deadlocks
Not knowing SQL Server resolves deadlocks automatically
Not knowing about error 1205 or retry logic

dialogue.text: What actually causes a deadlock?
A circular wait — transaction A holds a lock B needs while B holds one A needs. The lock monitor detects the cycle and kills the cheaper victim with error 1205.
How do you prevent them?
Access tables in a consistent order everywhere, keep transactions short, and index well so locks cover fewer rows; RCSI also removes a lot of reader-writer deadlocks.
They'll still happen occasionally. Then what?
The app should catch error 1205 and retry with backoff — in EF Core that's `EnableRetryOnFailure`.

followUpsQA.q: How do you set deadlock priority?
followUpsQA.a: SET DEADLOCK_PRIORITY LOW/HIGH to influence which session is chosen as victim.

followUpsQA.q: What is lock escalation and how can it cause deadlocks?
followUpsQA.a: Many row locks escalate to a table lock, widening contention and deadlock risk.

followUpsQA.q: How does RCSI remove common deadlocks?
followUpsQA.a: Readers use row versions instead of shared locks, eliminating reader-writer cycles.

followUpsQA.q: Where can you see the deadlock graph?
followUpsQA.a: The system_health Extended Events session captures it as an .xdl by default.

questions.q: Explain database normalization. What are 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF? When would you denormalize?

answer.oneLiner: Normalization is the process of structuring a relational database to reduce data redundancy and improve data integrity by organizing columns and tables so that each fact is stored in exactly one place. The three most common normal forms are 1NF, which requires atomic column values and no repeating groups; 2NF, which removes partial dependencies on composite keys; and 3NF, which removes transitive dependencies between non-key columns. Denormalization deliberately reverses some of this to improve read performance, which is why analytical data warehouses typically use wide, denormalized star schemas rather than fully normalized OLTP-style designs.
keyPoints: First Normal Form requires that every column holds a single atomic value, there are no repeating groups of columns, and the table has a defined primary key.

Second Normal Form requires that the table is already in First Normal Form and that every non-key column depends on the entire primary key, not just a part of it — this only matters when the primary key is composite.

Third Normal Form requires that the table is already in Second Normal Form and that no non-key column depends on another non-key column, meaning all non-key columns must depend directly on the primary key.

Denormalization is intentional redundancy introduced to speed up read-heavy workloads, such as reporting databases and data warehouse star schemas, where eliminating joins matters more than avoiding data duplication.
answer.detail: Examples: 1NF fixes a comma-separated phone column; 2NF moves ProductName out of OrderItems; 3NF moves DepartmentName out of Employees into Departments.

code.note: In practice, most OLTP databases target 3NF; data warehouses use star schemas (1-2NF)

redFlags: Can't give a concrete 2NF/3NF violation example
Thinking normalization always means better performance
Not knowing when to denormalize

dialogue.text: Explain 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF quickly.
1NF is atomic values with no repeating groups; 2NF removes partial dependency on part of a composite key; 3NF removes transitive dependency where a non-key column depends on another non-key column.
Give me a concrete 3NF violation.
Storing DepartmentName in the Employees table — it depends on DepartmentId, not on the employee key, so I'd move it into a Departments table.
When would you deliberately denormalize?
For read-heavy reporting or a data-warehouse star schema, where avoiding joins matters more than redundancy.

followUpsQA.q: What is BCNF?
followUpsQA.a: A stricter 3NF where every determinant is a candidate key.

followUpsQA.q: What is a star schema vs 3NF?
followUpsQA.a: Star schema is a denormalized fact-plus-dimensions model for analytics; 3NF minimizes redundancy for OLTP.

followUpsQA.q: How do indexed views avoid physical denormalization?
followUpsQA.a: They materialize a join/aggregate that SQL keeps in sync, giving denormalized speed without manual duplication.

followUpsQA.q: What's a 2NF violation example?
followUpsQA.a: Storing ProductName in OrderItems keyed by (OrderId, ProductId) — it depends only on ProductId.

questions.q: What is the difference between a primary key and a unique key? What is a foreign key?

answer.oneLiner: A primary key is the main identifier for a row — it must be unique, it cannot be NULL, and SQL Server creates a clustered index for it by default with a limit of one per table. A unique key also enforces uniqueness but it allows a single NULL value, can be created as a non-clustered index, and you can have multiple per table. A foreign key is a referential integrity constraint that enforces that every value in the child column either exists as a primary or unique key value in the parent table or is NULL.
keyPoints: A primary key column cannot be NULL, only one primary key is allowed per table, and it is backed by a clustered index by default.
A unique key allows one NULL value, you can have many unique keys per table, and the backing index is non-clustered by default.
A foreign key enforces referential integrity by requiring that the child column value either matches a value in the referenced parent column or is NULL.

SQL Server does not automatically create an index on a foreign key column in the child table — you need to add that index yourself to avoid full table scans when the parent row is deleted or to speed up joins.
answer.detail: Without an index on the child's FK column, deleting a parent row scans the whole child table to check dependents. FKs also support ON DELETE/UPDATE CASCADE, SET NULL, or RESTRICT.

code.note: Always index FK columns on child tables — SQL Server does NOT do this automatically

redFlags: Saying UNIQUE allows multiple NULLs
Not knowing FK columns aren't auto-indexed
Saying a table can have multiple clustered indexes

dialogue.text: Primary key versus unique key?
A PK is NOT NULL, one per table, and clustered by default; a unique key allows a single NULL, can appear many times per table, and is non-clustered by default.
And a foreign key?
It enforces that a child value exists in the parent table — or is NULL — keeping referential integrity.
Any indexing gotcha with foreign keys?
SQL Server doesn't auto-index FK columns, so deleting a parent can scan the whole child table — I always index FK columns myself.

followUpsQA.q: Can a PK column be NULL?
followUpsQA.a: No — a primary key is NOT NULL by definition.

followUpsQA.q: What happens deleting a parent without CASCADE?
followUpsQA.a: The delete is blocked if dependent child rows exist.

followUpsQA.q: Why index FK columns?
followUpsQA.a: To avoid full child-table scans on parent deletes and to speed joins.

followUpsQA.q: How many NULLs does a unique constraint allow?
followUpsQA.a: Just one in SQL Server, since two NULLs are treated as duplicate for uniqueness.

questions.q: How does a clustered index affect the physical storage of a table?

answer.oneLiner: A clustered index defines the physical order in which rows are stored on disk — the B-tree leaf level is not a separate structure pointing to the data, it literally is the data rows sorted by the key. This means range scans on the clustered key are fast because the rows are contiguous on disk, but inserts with random keys like GUIDs land in the middle of existing pages and cause page splits that fragment the data over time. Using an IDENTITY column as the clustered key avoids this because new rows always append to the last page.
keyPoints: The leaf level of a clustered index holds the actual data rows stored in key order — the index and the table storage are the same physical structure.
A table without a clustered index is called a heap, where rows have no defined order and non-clustered indexes locate them using a row identifier rather than the clustered key.
Every non-clustered index on the table carries the clustered key as its row locator, which means a wide clustered key inflates the size of every non-clustered index.

Inserting rows with random key values such as GUIDs causes page splits because new rows must be inserted in the middle of sorted pages, leading to fragmentation, whereas an IDENTITY value always appends to the end sequentially.
answer.detail: A wide clustered key bloats every non-clustered index. Fix fragmentation with REBUILD (>30%) or REORGANIZE (5-30%); both can update statistics.

code.snippet:
-- Check fragmentation
SELECT
    i.name AS IndexName,
    ips.avg_fragmentation_in_percent,
    ips.page_count
FROM sys.dm_db_index_physical_stats(DB_ID(), OBJECT_ID('Orders'), NULL, NULL, 'LIMITED') ips
JOIN sys.indexes i ON ips.object_id = i.object_id AND ips.index_id = i.index_id
WHERE ips.avg_fragmentation_in_percent > 5;

-- Rebuild (offline, resets fill factor)
ALTER INDEX ALL ON Orders REBUILD WITH (ONLINE = ON);

-- Reorganize (online, minimal locking)
ALTER INDEX IX_Orders_CustomerId ON Orders REORGANIZE;
code.note: Rebuild when fragmentation > 30%, reorganize when 5-30%; both update statistics

redFlags: Thinking the clustered index is separate from the table data
Not knowing non-clustered indexes carry the clustered key
Not knowing about page splits from random keys

dialogue.text: How does a clustered index change physical storage?
It orders the rows by the key — the B-tree leaf level *is* the data — so range scans on the key are fast and sequential.
What's the downside with random keys like GUIDs?
Inserts land in the middle of pages and cause page splits and fragmentation, whereas an IDENTITY column just appends to the end.
How do you deal with that fragmentation?
REORGANIZE between 5 and 30 percent, REBUILD above 30 — and tune fill factor to leave room for inserts.

followUpsQA.q: Why are GUIDs bad clustered keys on high-insert tables?
followUpsQA.a: Random ordering causes page splits and fragmentation on every insert.

followUpsQA.q: REBUILD vs REORGANIZE?
followUpsQA.a: REBUILD recreates the index (offline by default); REORGANIZE defragments online with less locking.

followUpsQA.q: How does fill factor affect page splits?
followUpsQA.a: Lower fill factor leaves free space per page, reducing splits at the cost of size.

followUpsQA.q: Why do non-clustered indexes carry the clustered key?
followUpsQA.a: They use it as the row locator, so a wide clustered key bloats every NC index.

questions.q: Explain key database design decisions: when to use surrogate vs natural keys, and how to design for scalability.

answer.oneLiner: A surrogate key is a system-generated identifier with no business meaning — typically an integer IDENTITY column — and it is almost always the better choice for a primary key because it is narrow, stable, and sequential, which is ideal for a clustered index. Natural keys like email addresses or product codes have real business meaning but they risk changing over time, being NULL, or turning out not to be truly unique, so the best practice is to use a surrogate primary key and enforce the natural key separately with a unique constraint. For tables that will grow to hundreds of millions of rows you need to think about scalability upfront through table partitioning by date, a consistent timestamp audit trail, and — for multi-tenant systems — sharding by tenant.
keyPoints: Surrogate keys have no business meaning, are narrow in size, stable over the life of the row, and sequential when using IDENTITY, which makes them efficient as clustered index keys.

Natural keys carry real-world meaning but are risky as primary keys because they can change, may not always be unique, and sometimes allow NULL — the safer approach is to enforce them with a separate UNIQUE constraint and use a surrogate as the primary key.

Partitioning large tables by a date column allows the optimizer to skip entire partitions when queries have date range filters, and it makes archiving old data almost instant through partition switching.

Adding CreatedAt and ModifiedAt timestamp columns from the very first migration is much cheaper than retrofitting them later, and for multi-tenant architectures sharding by tenant with a tenant identifier on every table is the standard approach to scaling out.
answer.detail: Keep the clustered key narrow since every non-clustered index carries it. Partition switching lets you archive old partitions in milliseconds.

code.note: Always add CreatedAt/ModifiedAt columns even if not immediately needed — retrofitting is expensive

redFlags: Using SSN or email as a clustered key
Not enforcing natural key uniqueness with surrogate PKs
Ignoring partitioning/archiving for tables over 100M rows

dialogue.text: Surrogate or natural key — what's your default?
A surrogate INT/BIGINT IDENTITY — it's narrow, stable, and has no business meaning that might change; I enforce the natural key with a separate UNIQUE constraint.
Why keep the clustered key narrow?
Because every non-clustered index carries the clustered key as its locator, so a wide key inflates all of them.
How do you design a huge Orders table for scale?
Partition by date for partition elimination and fast archiving via partition switch, and add CreatedAt/ModifiedAt from day one.

followUpsQA.q: What is partition elimination?
followUpsQA.a: The optimizer skips partitions that can't match the query's predicate.

followUpsQA.q: How would you design a multi-tenant Azure SQL DB?
followUpsQA.a: Shard or partition by tenant, with a tenant key on every table.

followUpsQA.q: Horizontal sharding vs vertical scaling trade-offs?
followUpsQA.a: Sharding scales out with app complexity; vertical scaling is simpler but capped by one machine.

followUpsQA.q: Why avoid natural keys like SSN as the PK?
followUpsQA.a: They can change, be NULL, or repeat, and they leak sensitive data into FKs.

questions.q: What is the difference between a primary key and a unique key in terms of NULL handling and index type?

answer.oneLiner: A primary key and a unique key both enforce that no two rows can have the same value, but they differ in two important ways. A primary key column is always NOT NULL and the constraint creates a clustered index by default, with only one allowed per table. A unique key permits a single NULL value in the column, creates a non-clustered index by default, and you can have many unique keys on the same table.
keyPoints: A primary key enforces NOT NULL, only one is allowed per table, and it is backed by a clustered index by default.
A unique key allows a single NULL value in the column, multiple unique keys are allowed on the same table, and the backing index is non-clustered by default.
Both primary keys and unique keys can be defined on multiple columns, making them composite keys.
A unique index and a unique constraint are functionally identical in SQL Server — a unique constraint is simply implemented as a unique index under the hood.

code.snippet:
CREATE TABLE Users (
    UserId    INT          IDENTITY CONSTRAINT PK_Users PRIMARY KEY CLUSTERED,
    Email     NVARCHAR(256) NOT NULL CONSTRAINT UQ_Users_Email UNIQUE NONCLUSTERED,
    Username  NVARCHAR(100) NOT NULL CONSTRAINT UQ_Users_Username UNIQUE
);
-- PK: clustered, not null
-- UNIQUE on Email: non-clustered, one NULL allowed (Email is NOT NULL here anyway)
code.note: Name your constraints explicitly for easier maintenance and error messages

redFlags: Saying UNIQUE allows multiple NULLs
Saying a table can have multiple clustered indexes
Not knowing PK is NOT NULL by definition

dialogue.text: On NULL handling, how do PK and unique key differ?
A primary key is NOT NULL, while a unique key permits a single NULL value in SQL Server.
And the default index type for each?
PK is clustered by default with one per table; a unique constraint is non-clustered by default and you can have many.
Is a unique constraint different from a unique index?
Functionally they're the same — the constraint is backed by a unique index under the hood.

followUpsQA.q: Can an FK reference a UNIQUE column instead of the PK?
followUpsQA.a: Yes — a foreign key can target any unique or primary key.

followUpsQA.q: How many unique indexes can a table have?
followUpsQA.a: Many — up to the non-clustered index limit of 999.

followUpsQA.q: What happens inserting two NULLs into a unique column?
followUpsQA.a: The second insert fails, since SQL Server treats two NULLs as duplicates for uniqueness.

followUpsQA.q: Why name constraints explicitly?
followUpsQA.a: For clearer error messages and easier scripting and maintenance.

questions.q: What is parameter sniffing and how do you deal with it?

answer.oneLiner: Parameter sniffing is when SQL Server compiles and caches a stored procedure's execution plan using the actual parameter values from the very first call, and then reuses that same plan for every subsequent call regardless of whether the new parameter values have very different selectivity. This is usually helpful because the plan is tailored to real data, but it becomes a problem when one customer has ten thousand orders and another has two, because the cached plan optimized for the large customer performs terribly for the small one and vice versa. The main fixes are using OPTION RECOMPILE to force a fresh plan on each execution, using OPTIMIZE FOR UNKNOWN to plan based on average statistics rather than a specific value, or using Query Store to force a known-good plan.
keyPoints: The execution plan is compiled and cached based on the parameter values present during the very first execution, and that same plan is reused for all subsequent calls.

Common fixes include OPTION RECOMPILE which generates a fresh plan per execution, OPTIMIZE FOR with a specific representative value, and OPTIMIZE FOR UNKNOWN which tells the optimizer to use average column statistics instead of the sniffed value.

Assigning the incoming parameter to a local variable before using it in the query disables parameter sniffing because the optimizer cannot see the local variable value at compile time and falls back to average statistics.
Broader solutions include using Query Store to identify and force a good plan, or splitting the procedure into multiple specialized procedures for different parameter ranges.

code.note: OPTION (RECOMPILE) is the simplest fix but trades CPU for a per-execution plan

redFlags: Not knowing the cached plan comes from the first parameters
Thinking parameter sniffing is always bad (it usually helps)
Slapping RECOMPILE on a high-frequency OLTP query without measuring

dialogue.text: A proc is fast for most inputs but crawls for one customer. What's your first suspicion?
Parameter sniffing — SQL cached a plan from the first parameter values, and that plan is wrong for a customer with very different selectivity.
How would you confirm and fix it?
Compare estimated vs actual rows in the plan; quick fixes are `OPTION (RECOMPILE)` for a fresh plan per call or `OPTIMIZE FOR UNKNOWN` to use the density average.
Any cost to RECOMPILE?
It trades CPU for a per-execution compile, so on a high-frequency OLTP query I'd measure first or force a good plan via Query Store instead.

followUpsQA.q: Why does a local variable disable parameter sniffing?
followUpsQA.a: The optimizer can't see its runtime value at compile time, so it uses an average estimate.

followUpsQA.q: How does Query Store help fix a regressed plan?
followUpsQA.a: It lets you force a previously good plan for the query.

followUpsQA.q: When is OPTION (RECOMPILE) too expensive?
followUpsQA.a: On high-frequency OLTP queries where the compile cost outweighs the better plan.

followUpsQA.q: Is parameter sniffing always bad?
followUpsQA.a: No — it usually helps by tailoring the plan; it only hurts on skewed data.

questions.q: What is a columnstore index and when would you use one?

answer.oneLiner: A columnstore index stores data column by column rather than row by row, which means all the values for a single column are stored together and can be compressed very efficiently because similar values sit next to each other. When an aggregation query like a SUM or GROUP BY runs, it only needs to read the few columns it cares about rather than every column of every row, and the SQL Server engine can process these in large batches of around nine hundred rows at a time instead of one row at a time, which makes analytical queries dramatically faster. For row-by-row OLTP operations like looking up a single order by ID, a traditional rowstore index is still the better choice.
keyPoints: Column-oriented storage combined with batch-mode execution — where the engine processes roughly 900 rows per operator call instead of one — makes columnstore indexes dramatically faster for large scans and aggregations.

Compression is much more effective than in rowstore because each column contains values of the same data type and similar magnitude, making run-length encoding and dictionary compression highly efficient.

A clustered columnstore index is ideal for analytical fact tables in a data warehouse, while a nonclustered columnstore index on an OLTP table enables hybrid transactional and analytical processing on live data.
Columnstore indexes perform poorly for single-row lookups and frequent small updates, so an OLTP table with those access patterns should stick with rowstore indexes.

code.note: Columnstore shines on SELECT SUM/COUNT/GROUP BY over large tables, not on point lookups

redFlags: Using columnstore for an OLTP point-lookup table
Not knowing columnstore compresses per-column
Confusing columnstore with covering indexes

dialogue.text: When would you choose a columnstore index?
For analytical workloads — big aggregations and scans over millions of rows — because column-oriented storage plus batch-mode execution makes those queries dramatically faster.
Why does it compress so well?
Similar values sit together within each column, so run-length and dictionary compression are very effective.
Where would columnstore be a bad fit?
On an OLTP table with single-row seeks and frequent small updates — rowstore is far better there; you'd only add a nonclustered columnstore for HTAP.

followUpsQA.q: Rowstore vs columnstore trade-offs?
followUpsQA.a: Rowstore wins point lookups/OLTP; columnstore wins scans/aggregates.

followUpsQA.q: What is batch-mode execution?
followUpsQA.a: Processing ~900 rows per operator call instead of one, cutting CPU on big scans.

followUpsQA.q: What is HTAP and how does nonclustered columnstore enable it?
followUpsQA.a: Hybrid transactional/analytical — a NCCI on an OLTP table serves analytics on live data.

followUpsQA.q: What is a rowgroup in columnstore?
followUpsQA.a: A compressed segment of up to ~1M rows, the unit of columnstore storage.

questions.q: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic locking?

answer.oneLiner: Pessimistic locking assumes conflicts are likely, so it acquires a lock on the data as soon as it is read and holds that lock until the transaction commits, forcing any other session that wants the same row to wait. Optimistic locking assumes conflicts are rare, so it reads data without taking any lock and only checks at write time whether the row has changed since it was read — typically by comparing a row version or timestamp column. If the check fails because someone else changed the row in the meantime, the operation returns a conflict and the caller can re-read and retry.
keyPoints: Pessimistic locking holds locks such as UPDLOCK or HOLDLOCK from the moment the row is read until the transaction commits, which is safe but blocks other sessions and increases the risk of deadlocks.

Optimistic locking reads data freely without any lock and then validates a version column or rowversion value at the time of the update to detect whether another session changed the row in the interim.

Optimistic locking provides better concurrency under low contention because readers and writers do not block each other, while pessimistic locking is more appropriate for high-contention scenarios or critical sections where conflicts are frequent.

When an optimistic update detects a conflict because the row changed since it was read, the standard responses are to re-read the latest values and retry, or to return a 409 Conflict error to the caller.

code.note: Optimistic locking is the default in EF Core via a rowversion concurrency token

redFlags: Confusing the two strategies
